results.possibleIdentity: Morton Leonard Heilig (1926–1997) was a pioneering American filmmaker, designer, and inventor, widely regarded as the "Father of Virtual Reality." He was active in New York City during the late 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s. In 1963, he founded Sensorama, Inc. (also referred to as Sensorama Incorporated) in New York to develop and market his multi-sensory simulator, the Sensorama, which he patented in 1962. This device offered an immersive "experience theater" through 3D visuals, stereo sound, wind, vibrations, and scents. He also patented the Telesphere Mask (1960), the first head-mounted display, and the Experience Theater (1969), a multi-person sensory booth. As a filmmaker and designer, he directed award-winning works such as "Assembly Line" (1961) and the feature film "Once" (1974). He is historically associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, a collaboration between artists and engineers that gained prominence in the late 1960s, and his work is frequently cited alongside E.A.T. figures like Billy Klüver and Stan VanDerBeek for its integration of technology and sensory art.
